Return Policy

We offer a 30-day hassle-free return on all SlopeSense products. If you are not completely satisfied, return it in original condition within 30 days for a full refund or exchange. Items must be unused and in original packaging. Shipping costs for returns are covered by SlopeSense on first return. To start a return, contact us at returns@slopesense.com or use the contact page.

Who we are

Built by skiers.
For skiers.

Est. 2025 | West Vancouver, BC
Our story

Why we built this

SlopeSense started with a simple frustration: spending a full day on the mountain with cold, aching feet that no amount of extra socks could fix. After watching too many skiers cut their days short because of discomfort rather than skill or energy, we asked a simple question. Why hasn't anyone actually solved this?

We looked at what existed. Heated insoles. Custom liners. Compression socks. All decent in isolation. None of them connected. None of them smart. None of them adapting to what your feet actually needed in real time.

So we built SlopeSense from scratch. A sensor system that combines heating, pressure adjustment, massage, and circulation monitoring into one product you control from your phone. The goal was simple: more time on the mountain, less time thinking about your feet.

Mission

Make every skier's day on the mountain more comfortable through smart technology that actually works.

Innovation

We refuse to accept that ski boot discomfort is just part of skiing. Every problem has a smarter solution.

Accessibility

Premium comfort should not be limited to elite athletes. SlopeSense is built for every skier.

Durability

Mountain conditions are brutal. Everything we build is engineered to handle them without compromise.

Got questions?

Frequently
Asked Questions

SlopeSense is designed as a universal liner insert, meaning it fits the vast majority of ski boot liners on the market. The sensor system sits inside your existing liner and does not require you to buy a new boot. Most standard alpine ski boots from brands like Salomon, Atomic, Rossignol, and Lange are compatible.
The SlopeSense removable battery pack lasts approximately 8 hours with heating and all features running simultaneously. With heating off or on low, you can expect 12 to 14 hours. The pack charges fully in around 2 hours via USB-C and can be swapped mid-day if needed. A spare battery pack is available as an add-on accessory.
Yes. The SlopeSense sensor system is IP67 rated, meaning it can handle full submersion in up to 1 metre of water for 30 minutes. The charging port has a protective seal that must be closed during skiing. We have tested the system extensively in wet snow and wet boot conditions.
Yes. SlopeSense has a default comfort mode that activates automatically without the app. It maintains moderate heating and standard fit settings. For full control over pressure zones, heating levels, massage modes, and custom presets, the companion app on iOS or Android is required. The app is free to download.
We offer a 30-day hassle-free return on all orders. Items must be returned in original, unused condition with original packaging. SlopeSense covers the shipping cost on your first return. Refunds are processed within 5 business days of receiving the return. Contact returns@slopesense.com to start the process.
SlopeSense is currently optimized for ski boots. That said, customers have used the system successfully in snowboard boots with compatible liners. Official snowboard support is planned for the next generation product.
The massage mode uses the same micro air bladder system as the pressure adjustment feature. In massage mode, the bladders cycle through pulsing inflation and deflation patterns that mimic compression massage. You can activate this manually through the app or set it to trigger automatically during lift rides when movement is minimal.

Comfort and Performance

Why Your Ski Boots Might Be Ruining Your Day on the Mountain

May 2025 | 5 min read

Most skiers blame their technique when something feels off on the mountain. Their turns aren't clean, their legs are burning after two runs, or they just feel disconnected from their skis. The default assumption is that more practice will fix it. A lot of the time, the real problem is their boots.

Ski boot fit is one of the most overlooked factors in skiing performance and comfort. A boot that is too loose allows the foot to move around inside the liner, which reduces energy transfer and causes blisters. A boot that is too tight cuts off circulation, which leads to numbness, cold feet, and fatigue much earlier in the day than it should (Harding, 2021).

The circulation problem

Skiing in tight boots is one of the most common causes of cold feet on the mountain, and it has nothing to do with the temperature outside. When a boot is buckled too aggressively, it compresses the blood vessels in the foot and reduces circulation. Your body responds by pulling warm blood away from the extremities to protect the core, which leaves your feet cold and numb even on mild days.

Research on athletic footwear and circulation has consistently shown that dynamic pressure adjustment throughout activity, rather than a single static fit at the start, leads to significantly better blood flow and less fatigue over time (Nigg et al., 2015).

What traditional boots get wrong

The fundamental problem with traditional ski boots is that they are designed for a static fit. You buckle them at the trailhead and that is it until lunch. But your feet change throughout the day. They swell from activity, they contract in the cold, and the liner compresses over hours of use. A boot that fit perfectly at 9am may be too loose or too tight by noon.

Brands like Therm-ic and Hotronic have addressed the heating problem with heated insoles and battery packs, but nobody has addressed the fit problem in real time until now.

The case for smart boot technology

A system that monitors foot temperature and pressure throughout the day and adjusts in real time solves problems that no amount of custom fitting or aftermarket insoles can fully address. When the boot adapts to your foot instead of your foot adapting to the boot, you ski better, you last longer, and you enjoy the mountain more.

That is the idea behind SlopeSense. Not a replacement for a good fitting boot, but a system that makes any boot smarter.

Harding, J. (2021). Footwear fit and lower limb performance in alpine skiing. Journal of Sports Science, 39(4), 112-119.
Nigg, B., Nurse, M., and Stefanyshyn, D. (2015). Shoe inserts and orthotics for sport and physical activity. Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise, 31(7), S421-S428.

Technology

How Smart Wearables Are Changing Winter Sports

April 2025 | 7 min read

The ski industry has always been gear-driven. Skiers obsess over bindings, boot stiffness, and ski geometry in ways that most other sports communities simply do not. So it makes sense that smart wearable technology has found a natural home on the mountain.

Over the last five years, a wave of connected devices has hit the ski market. GPS trackers that log your vertical meters, heart rate monitors built into helmets, and apps that track your runs in real time. Some of it is genuinely useful. Some of it is just marketing. Here is how to tell the difference.

What actually helps performance

The most useful wearables in skiing are the ones that solve real physical problems rather than just collecting data. Heart rate and exertion tracking helps recreational skiers understand their limits and avoid overtraining. GPS and speed data can help with pacing on long days. These tools have clear, direct applications.

The global smart wearable market in sports reached $19.6 billion USD in 2023 and is projected to grow at over 15% annually through 2030, driven largely by demand in outdoor and action sports (Grand View Research, 2024).

Where the gap is

For all the innovation in ski tech, footwear comfort has been largely ignored. The boot is the most critical interface between the skier and the ski, and yet the technology inside most boots has not changed meaningfully in decades. Heated insoles exist, but they do not adapt. Pressure adjustment exists, but it requires manually stopping and re-buckling.

The next wave of meaningful ski wearables will not be on your wrist or your helmet. It will be inside your boot.

Grand View Research. (2024). Smart wearable market size, share and trends analysis report. Retrieved from grandviewresearch.com

Sustainability

The Future of Sustainable Ski Gear

March 2025 | 6 min read

The ski industry has a complicated relationship with the environment. Mountains are the product. Climate change is the existential threat. And the gear that skiers buy, from boots to bindings to base layers, is often made with materials and processes that contribute to the very problem threatening the sport's future.

According to a 2022 report by the Outdoor Industry Association, the apparel and footwear sector generates approximately 10% of global carbon emissions, with a significant portion coming from synthetic materials used in performance gear (Outdoor Industry Association, 2022).

What responsible brands are doing

The shift toward sustainability in ski gear is real, but it is uneven. Some brands are making meaningful changes. Others are greenwashing. The difference usually comes down to whether sustainability is built into the product design from the start or applied as an afterthought through marketing.

Meaningful approaches include using recycled or bio-based materials in liners and shells, designing products for longevity and repairability rather than planned obsolescence, and building take-back programs so that end-of-life gear does not end up in landfill.

The durability argument

One of the most sustainable things a gear company can do is make something that lasts. A product that lasts ten years has a fraction of the environmental impact of one that lasts two. At SlopeSense, durability is a core design principle, not just for sustainability reasons, but because skiers deserve gear they can rely on season after season.

Outdoor Industry Association. (2022). Outdoor industry sustainability report. Retrieved from outdoorindustry.org

The Summit Session | Episode 1

Why Comfort Sells: Building SlopeSense

Approx. 5 min | Business, Marketing, E-Commerce

Host: Griffin Bremner, Founder of SlopeSense. Guest: James Holloway, Sports Tech Journalist and longtime ski industry observer.

Griffin
Welcome to The Summit Session. I am Griffin, founder of SlopeSense, and today I am joined by James Holloway who covers the ski and outdoor sports tech space. James, thanks for being here.
James
Happy to be here. SlopeSense caught my attention when I first heard about it because the pitch is so simple. Ski boots are uncomfortable. Let us fix that. It sounds obvious but nobody has actually done it well.
Griffin
That is exactly where it came from. I had a day on the mountain where my feet were just destroyed by noon. Cold, cramped, circulation cut off. And I started looking at what existed. Heated insoles, yes. Custom fitting, sure. But nothing that actually adapted in real time to what your foot needed. That gap was the whole idea.
James
From a market perspective, what does the competitive landscape look like? Because brands like Therm-ic and Hotronic have been doing heated boots for years.
Griffin
They solve one part of the problem. Heating. But they do not do pressure adjustment, they do not do massage or recovery, and none of them are connected to an app in a meaningful way. SlopeSense is the first product that combines all of those things into a single system. That is the differentiation.
James
Let us talk about the e-commerce side. Building a direct-to-consumer brand in a market dominated by big outdoor retailers, that is not an easy play. What is the strategy?
Griffin
Direct-to-consumer lets us control the full customer experience and the story. When you buy SlopeSense on our website, you are not getting a generic product description from a retailer who sells a thousand other brands. You are getting the full context of why this product exists, what it does, and how to get the most out of it. That relationship matters, especially with a tech product that has a bit of a learning curve.
James
What about the price point? $389 CAD is not nothing for a boot accessory.
Griffin
It is a premium product. But when you compare it to what people spend on ski gear generally, it is actually pretty reasonable. A decent ski boot runs anywhere from $400 to over $1,000. If SlopeSense makes that boot significantly more comfortable and extends your day on the mountain, the value is there. We are not competing with a $30 insole. We are competing with a whole category of partial solutions, and we are combining all of them for less.
James
Last question. What does success look like for SlopeSense in year one?
Griffin
Honest answer? Getting the product in front of real skiers and getting real feedback. Revenue matters, but in year one the most valuable thing is learning whether the product actually delivers on the promise. If people are skiing longer, feeling better, and coming back to buy a second pair for a friend, that is the signal we want. Everything else follows from that.
James
That is a good answer. Thanks for having me on.
Griffin
Thanks James. And thanks to everyone listening to The Summit Session. Next episode we are getting into SEO strategy for niche e-commerce brands. See you then.
